record_identity.title: Machine-readable human consent signals for AI use of identity and creative work

summary: Proposal to develop CAM governance treatment for machine-readable human consent signals governing AI use of creative works, name, image, likeness, identity, voice, performance, and related human expressive material.
proposal_rationale: Public consent registries and machine-readable permissions may become important governance infrastructure for AI systems that ingest, train on, reproduce, simulate, transform, or commercially use human creative work, identity, likeness, voice, performance, or expressive material. CAM requires a governance treatment capable of distinguishing consent signalling, rights assertion, provenance, identity integrity, creator control, machine readability, interoperability, enforcement limits, and the risk that consent registries become fragmented, inaccessible, commercially captured, or ignored by AI systems.

source_records.source_title: Variety ANZ — Cate Blanchett Co-Founds RSL Media, a Non-Profit to Address Consent Around AI Usage
source_records.system_or_product: RSL Media / RSL Human Consent Standard
source_records.deployment_context: Public reporting on a non-profit consent framework and registry for AI use of creative work, name, image, likeness, and human identity-related material.
source_records.source_context: The article reports that RSL Media is developing a free public registry and human consent framework intended to allow people to declare permissions for AI use of creative work, identity, and likeness, including machine-readable consent signalling.
source_records.relevance_note: Source supports a CAM proposal for governance treatment of machine-readable human consent signals, creator rights, identity integrity, and AI-use permissions.

system_context.deployment_context: Consent registry and machine-readable signalling infrastructure for declaring permitted or prohibited AI use of human creative work, name, image, likeness, identity, voice, performance, or related expressive material.
system_context.user_role: creator / performer / rights-holder / public registrant / AI-system operator / policy reviewer
system_context.affected_population: artists, performers, writers, creators, public figures, private individuals, rights-holders, AI developers, platforms, regulators, and registry users
system_context.interface_surface: public consent registry / machine-readable permissions interface

jurisdictional_context.sector: creative industries / AI governance / digital identity / rights-management infrastructure

proposal_scope.scope_summary: Develop CAM governance treatment for machine-readable human consent signals and registries governing AI use of creative works, identity, likeness, voice, performance, and related expressive material.
automation_components: validator checks for source-link provenance where consent-signal records are cited
possible registry field support for consent_signal_url, rights_signal_type, and machine_readability_status
interface_components: possible public display support for consent-signal infrastructure records
possible UX labels distinguishing evidence source, rights signal, and governance proposal

suggested_insertion_points: Develop a CAM clause or appendix addressing machine-readable consent signals for AI use of human identity, likeness, voice, performance, and creative work.
Assess whether the consent-signal governance treatment belongs primarily in IDENTITY, ECONOMICS, LATTICE, OPERATIONS, or a cross-domain instrument.
Create a crosswalk between consent signals, provenance signals, licence signals, rights-reservation signals, and human identity-integrity constraints.
Clarify that a registry signal is governance-relevant evidence of asserted permissions or restrictions, but not automatically dispositive of legal rights, consent validity, or enforcement outcome.
Assess whether VIGIL source records should support explicit fields for consent registries, machine-readable rights signals, and registry-verification status.
template_changes: Consider adding optional source_records fields for consent_signal_url, rights_signal_type, asserted_permission_state, and registry_verification_status.
schema_changes: Consider optional schema support for machine-readable consent or rights-signal infrastructure references.
validator_changes: Consider validator warnings where a record cites rights or consent infrastructure without distinguishing primary evidence, legal instrument, registry assertion, and governance interpretation.
interface_changes: Consider displaying consent-signal infrastructure as a distinct public-governance category if further records emerge.
migration_concerns: Avoid treating external consent registries as legally conclusive without jurisdictional analysis.
Avoid conflating copyright permission, likeness consent, voice consent, biometric identity, performance rights, and training-data permission.
Avoid requiring vulnerable or low-resource individuals to register in private systems before their rights are recognised.
dependencies: Review cross-domain interfaces with IDENTITY, ECONOMICS, LATTICE, RELATION, OPERATIONS, SECURITY, MENTIS, Annex I, Annex K, and Annex L.

external_relevance.external_summary: The proposal is relevant to platforms, AI developers, creators, performers, rights-holders, regulators, standards bodies, provenance initiatives, registry designers, and researchers working on AI consent, identity, likeness, copyright, synthetic media, and machine-readable rights signalling.

standards.title: Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity
standards.relevance_note: Relevant as adjacent provenance infrastructure. Consent signalling should be distinguished from provenance signalling but may require interoperable metadata treatment.

standards.title: W3C Verifiable Credentials
standards.relevance_note: Relevant as a possible technical reference point for verifiable claims, credentials, or registry assertions, without implying that consent registries must use this standard.

standards.title: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 — Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system
standards.relevance_note: Relevant as an AI management-system standard for organizational governance, documented controls, risk treatment, lifecycle management, and responsible AI use.

standards.title: NIST Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework 1.0
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology
standards.relevance_note: Relevant as a voluntary AI risk-management framework for incorporating trustworthiness, mapping, measuring, managing, and governance considerations into AI systems.

cam_internal.governance_layer: identity integrity / creative rights / consent signalling / provenance / economic governance
cam_internal.routing_note: Proposal record for assessing machine-readable consent infrastructure for AI use of creative work, human identity, likeness, voice, and performance. This record does not claim implementation and does not create a patch note.

next_action: Review whether CAM requires a dedicated cross-domain clause, appendix, or proposal addressing machine-readable human consent signals for AI systems, including interoperability with identity integrity, provenance, creative labour, platform governance, and AI-system compliance obligations.
migration_notes: Created under the clean VIGIL Proposal JSON template.
Uses source_records as the canonical source-evidence block.
No patch note is inferred by this proposal record.
Prepared as VIGIL-2026-PROP-0008 for insertion into vigil/records/proposals/2026/.
